document_id: 2nd qtr 2017 gas 1278 ifta fuel tax rates

findings.description: Page header/title hierarchy is weakened in the HTML.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, 'GAS-1278' and the long title block are tightly grouped and visually prominent at the top. In the HTML, the same content is split across multiple lines/sections with more whitespace and lighter visual emphasis, making the title area less compact.

findings.description: The PDF contains the tax-rate table split across two pages, but the HTML merges both pages into a single table, changing the original page/table structure.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows a full table beginning with 'STATE OR PROVINCE ST/ABBREV' and ending with 'NV Nevada'. PDF page 2 starts a new table with the same header and continues from 'NB New Brunswick' through 'WY Wyoming'. HTML contains only one <table> with all rows from 'AL Alabama' through 'WY Wyoming', instead of preserving the two-page table split.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy is not an accurate reflection of the source document structure because page-level repeated headers are promoted to semantic headings, creating multiple document headings and nesting issues.
findings.evidence:
HTML uses '

GAS-1278

' followed by '

International Fuel Tax Agreement (IFTA) Fuel Tax Rates for 2nd Quarter 2017 Returns

' and then another page header '

2nd Quarter 2017 IFTA Tax Rates

' for page 2. In the PDF, '2nd Quarter 2017 IFTA Tax Rates' is a page-2 table heading, not a separate top-level section of the document.

findings.description: Header content is duplicated in the HTML in a way that does not correspond cleanly to the source document's logical structure.
findings.evidence: The HTML includes both the first-page header block ('GAS-1278', 'International Fuel Tax Agreement (IFTA) Fuel Tax Rates for 2nd Quarter 2017 Returns') and a second-page header block ('Page 2, Gas 1278, Web, 6-17', '2nd Quarter 2017 IFTA Tax Rates') before a single combined table. In the PDF these belong to separate pages framing separate table instances; in HTML they appear consecutively ahead of one merged table, which duplicates page-level heading content.
